name: Greco-Roman Museum
description: Location & Significance: Located in Alexandria, the Graeco-Roman Museum is a premier archaeological institution. It houses an extensive collection of over 40,000 objects, including sculptures, intricate mosaics, woodwork, and rare coins. The museum provides a profound view of the Greco-Roman civilization and its deep interactions with ancient Egypt. History & Foundation: Early Beginnings (1892): The museum was initially established in a humble five-room apartment within a small building on Rosetta Street (later known as Avenue Canope, and presently Horriya Street).

name: Qaitbay Citadel
description: Location & Significance: The Citadel of Qaitbay is widely considered one of the most vital defensive strongholds not only in Egypt but along the entire Mediterranean coast. Located on the eastern point of the northern tip of Pharos Island, at the mouth of the Eastern Harbour in Alexandria, it formulated a crucial part of the city's fortification system in the 15th century AD. The Lighthouse Connection: The citadel was erected on the exact site of the ancient Lighthouse of Alexandria (one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World). The lighthouse survived and functioned for centuries post-Arab conquest, seeing restoration under Ahmed Ibn Tulun (around 880 AD).

name: Royal Jewelry Museum Alexandria
description: Location & Significance: Located in the prestigious Zizenia neighborhood of Alexandria, the Royal Jewelry Museum is an exquisite art and history museum housed in the former palace of Princess Fatma Al-Zahra'. The building's magnificent halls display an inestimable collection of jewels and jewelry belonging to the Muhammad Ali Dynasty (which ruled Egypt for nearly 150 years, from 1805 until the 1952 revolution). The museum's rooms and lobbies also exhibit beautiful 19th-century paintings, statues, and decorative arts. History of the Palace: Construction (1919): Built in 1919, the palace's western wing was completed by Princess Fatima's mother prior to her death (when Fatima was 18 years old).

name: Alexandria Bibliotheca
description: Location & Significance: Situated on the Mediterranean shoreline near the city's ancient harbor, the Bibliotheca Alexandrina is a massive global library and cultural center. It was built as a commemoration and modern revival of the ancient Library of Alexandria, which was once the largest repository of knowledge in the ancient world before its tragic loss. History & Foundation (The Modern Library): The Concept: The idea to revive the library dates back to 1974, initiated by Alexandria University. The project gained massive momentum through the support of the Egyptian government and a vital partnership with UNESCO.

name: Alexandria National Museum
description: Location & Significance: The Alexandria National Museum is situated on Tariq Al-Horreya Street (formerly Rue Fouad). It is a vital cultural landmark that narrates the expansive history of Alexandria and Egypt. The museum houses approximately 1,800 artifacts, most of which were meticulously selected and transferred from other major Egyptian museums to provide a cohesive historical narrative. History & Foundation: The Building: The museum is located in a magnificent Italianate mansion dating back to 1926.

name: Serapeum of Alexandria
description: Location & Significance: Situated on a prominent rocky plateau, the Serapeum historically overlooked the Mediterranean Sea to the north and Lake Mareotis to the south. By all accounts, it was the largest and most magnificent temple in the Greek quarter of Alexandria. The sanctuary was dedicated to Serapis, the syncretic Greco-Egyptian god made the protector of Alexandria. Crucially, the temple precinct also housed the famous "daughter library," an offshoot collection of the Great Library of Alexandria.

name: Catacombs of Kom El Shoqafa
description: Location & Significance: Located in the western necropolis of Alexandria, the Catacombs of Kom El Shoqafa are widely considered one of the "Seven Wonders of the Middle Ages." This historical archaeological site is a spectacular showcase of cultural syncretism, seamlessly merging ancient Egyptian, Hellenistic, and early Imperial Roman artistic and architectural traditions. History, Discovery & Name: Construction: Tunneled deep into solid bedrock during the age of the Antonine emperors (2nd century CE), the facility was utilized as a burial chamber from the 2nd to the 4th centuries. It is believed to have been originally intended for a single family before being mysteriously expanded to house numerous individuals. Discovery (1900): The catacombs were lost to history until their accidental rediscovery in 1900, when a donkey fell through the main access shaft.

name: Catacombs of Mostafa Kamel
description: Location & Significance: Located adjacent to the officers' housing area in the Mustafa Kamel district of Alexandria, this Ptolemaic necropolis consists of four main tombs entirely carved out of natural bedrock. Tombs 1 and 2 are completely subterranean, whereas Tombs 3 and 4 are partially elevated above ground level. Each tomb in this complex was designed to accommodate multiple burials. History & Discovery: The tombs date back to the late 3rd century and early 2nd century BC.

name: Antoniades Palace
description: Location & Area: Located near the Mahmoudia Canal at the southern entrance of Alexandria, the palace is surrounded by a sprawling 48 hectares (120 acres) of lush greenery. The estate is divided into several sections, including the Antoniadis Garden, the Flower Garden, the Nouzaha (Nuzha) Municipal Garden, and the Zoological and Botanical Gardens. Sir John Antoniadis: The palace is named after Sir John Antoniadis (1818–1895), an Alexandrian Greek born in Lemnos. He acquired French citizenship while conducting business in Marseille, served as the president of the Greek Community in Alexandria, and acted as the consul general of Belgium.

name: Montaza Palace
description: Location & Significance: Montaza Palace (Asr el Montaza) is a magnificent complex comprising palaces, a museum, and extensive royal gardens located in the Montaza district, east of central Alexandria. Built on a low plateau, the complex offers breathtaking views overlooking a beach on the Mediterranean Sea. History & Construction Phases: Salamlek Palace (1892): This was the very first structure built on the expansive Montaza grounds. It was constructed by Khedive Abbas II, who was the last ruler of the Muhammad Ali Dynasty to hold the title of Khedive over Egypt and Sudan.

name: Sidi Morsi Abu al Abbas Mosque
description: Location & Significance: Located in the historic Anfoushi neighbourhood on the northern coast of Alexandria, this mosque acts as a prominent place of worship, a mausoleum, and a Sufi shrine. The 13th-century mosque honors the revered 13th-century mystic Abu'l Abbas al-Mursi, who is buried there alongside members of the Ashraf family. It is uniquely situated adjacent to the Al-Busiri Mosque and the Sidi Yaqut Al-Arsh Mosque, with all three facing the same central sahn (courtyard). History & Construction Phases: Early Years (1286–1307 CE): Abu'l Abbas al-Mursi passed away in 1286 CE and was buried in a small cemetery in Bab al-Bahr.

name: Alexandria Ancient Roman Theater
description: Location & Significance: The ancient Roman Amphitheatre is located in the Kom El Deka district in the heart of central Alexandria. Constructed in the early 4th century AD during the Roman era, it is a rare and significant archaeological monument in Egypt (second only to the Pelusium theatre east of the Suez Canal), reflecting the rich cultural and educational life of ancient Alexandria. Discovery & True Function: Accidental Discovery (1960): The structure was discovered entirely by chance in 1960 by a Polish archaeological mission that was clearing earth in search of the lost tomb of Alexander the Great. Excavation work at the site took roughly 30 years.

name: St Mark's Cathedral Alexandria
description: Location & Significance: Saint Mark's Coptic Orthodox Cathedral is located in Alexandria and stands as the historical seat of the Pope of Alexandria, the head of the Coptic Orthodox Church. It is a site of immense spiritual significance, as it is believed to be built on the exact location in Baucalis where St. Mark the Evangelist (author of the second Gospel) founded the first church in Egypt around AD 67–68. Early History & The Relics of St.

name: Taposiris Magna
description: History & Foundation: Established by Pharaoh Ptolemy II Philadelphus between 280 and 270 BC, its name translates to the "great tomb of Osiris." Located about 30 miles west of Alexandria in the coastal town of Borg El Arab, it was an important religious center for the Khoiak festival. The ancient historian Callisthenes noted that Alexander the Great visited the city on his way to the Siwa Oasis. The settlement remained continuously occupied from the 2nd century BC until the 7th century AD. Commercial Importance: Situated on a navigable arm of the now-dried ancient Lake Mariout, the city served as a crucial port facilitating trade between Egypt and Libya.

name: Shatby Cemetery
description: Location & Area: Situated in the heart of modern Alexandria (in the Shatby district), the cemetery occupies a significant area of 3,500 square meters. History & Foundation: The use of this cemetery dates back to the late 4th century BCE, beginning just a couple of decades after the foundation of Alexandria by Alexander the Great. It remained in continuous use as a burial site for the following three centuries. Discovery & Restoration: The site was unearthed during systematic archaeological excavations conducted between 1904 and 1910.

name: Anfushi Tombs
description: Location & Significance: Located near the Eastern Harbor and the entrance to the Ras El-Tin Palace in Alexandria, the Anfushy Necropolis is a magnificent archaeological site. It perfectly illustrates the interaction and fusion between Pharaonic art and Greco-Roman religious cultures. History & Foundation: The tombs date back to the period between the mid-2nd century BCE and the mid-1st century BCE (late Ptolemaic to early Roman eras). Built according to the Oikos (house) plan, these tombs were constructed for a newly emerged elite class in Alexandria, comprising either individuals of mixed descent or Hellenized Egyptians.

name: Ras el Soda Temple
description: Location & Significance: The Ras el-Soda Temple is a significant Roman-Egyptian religious structure in Alexandria. Originally located in the Ras el-Soda district, it was relocated in the early 1990s by the Supreme Council for Antiquities to the Chatby Garden on Horreya Street to protect it from rising groundwater. History & Foundation: Dating back to the 2nd century CE (Roman era), the temple was a private foundation established by a Roman citizen named Isidoros. He had the temple erected as a dedicated offering to the Egyptian goddess Isis, thanking her for healing his foot after a severe fall from his horse-driven carriage.

name: Cavafy Museum
description: Location & Significance: The museum is situated in the heart of Alexandria (Attarin district), near the Alexandria Opera House on Fouad St. and Safiya Zaghloul St. The street's name has evolved over time: known as Lepsius Street during Cavafy's lifetime, it was later renamed Sharm El Sheikh Street, and finally designated as Cavafy Street upon the museum's opening. For Cavafy, the location was deeply symbolic, situated between the Greek Orthodox Patriarchate (which he called the "Temple of the Soul") and the city's bordellos (the "Temple of the Body" or "Flesh").

name: Monastery of Saint Mina
description: Location & Significance: Belonging to the Coptic Orthodox Church of Alexandria, this monastery is located in the Western Desert near Alexandria. The massive modern monastery is built closely adjacent to the ruins of "Abu Mena," the original pilgrimage site dating back to the mid-7th century. History & Burial: Following Saint Mina's execution, his sister brought his body to a church in Alexandria. When the era of persecution ended, during the papacy of Pope Athanasius of Alexandria, an angel appeared to the Pope instructing him to load the saint's body onto a camel and head into the Western Desert.

name: Sacred Heart Church
description: Significance & Denomination: The Sacred Heart Church in Alexandria is a prominent Catholic church primarily visited by the city's Latin Catholic community. Its patron saint is St. Francis, an Italian monk born in 1181, whose name is famously lent to the many Franciscan schools established worldwide. Interior Design & Religious Art: The church is a highly recommended destination due to its breathtaking religious art.

name: Saint Catherine Cathedral
description: Location & Significance: St. Catherine's Cathedral is a significant Roman Catholic cathedral situated in Alexandria. It serves as the official seat of the Apostolic Vicariate of Alexandria of Egypt and also functions as the primary church for the city's Franciscan monastery. History & Foundation: The Franciscan presence in Alexandria traces its roots back to the 17th century, primarily catering to European pilgrims traveling to the Holy Land.

name: Terbanh Mosque
description: Location: The mosque is situated in the historic El Gomrok neighbourhood on the northern coast of Alexandria, specifically located on Faransa Street. History & Foundation: The mosque was completed around 1685 AD by Hajj Ibrahim Terbana, a wealthy Moroccan merchant. To preserve its historical and religious integrity, a major restoration project began in 2010. An investment of 23 million EGP was allocated to the project, aimed at protecting the sanctity of the mosque and stopping any structural encroachment by tourists and worshippers.

name: El Nabi Daniel Mosque
description: Location & Historical Significance: Located in Alexandria, the mosque is steeped in mystery. It is widely speculated to be the lost site of the Tomb of Alexander the Great. The mosque's name is erroneously attributed to the biblical Prophet Daniel; in reality, it honors Muhammad Daniyal al-Mawsili, a Kurdish Muslim scholar of the Shafi'i school who is buried there. History: 1790: The original building was constructed over the grave of the scholar Muhammad Daniyal al-Mawsili.

name: Ras el-Tin Palace
description: Location & Significance: Ras El Tin Palace (meaning "Cape Fig Palace") is beautifully situated on the Mediterranean coast in Alexandria's Ras el-Tin quarter, directly overlooking the Western Harbour. Built on a promontory that was historically the site of the ancient island of Pharos, it holds the title of the oldest royal Egyptian palace still in use. Today, it serves as an official residence for the President of Egypt and was officially registered on the list of Islamic and Coptic antiquities in 2015. The palace derives its name from the fig trees that originally grew on the site.

name: Museum of Fine Arts
description: Location & Significance: Located in the historic Moharam Bek neighborhood, this museum is a premier destination for Egyptian and Middle-Eastern fine art. It houses an extensive collection reflecting world-class artistic movements, including Baroque, Romanticism, Rococo, and Orientalism. Additionally, it features noteworthy examples of carving, printing, and sculpture by both Egyptian and European masters. History & Development: Early Beginnings (1904): The museum’s roots trace back to a gift from Edward Farid Heim, who donated 210 artworks to the Alexandria Municipality.

name: Mahmoud Said Museum
description: Location & Significance: Housed in the artist’s beautiful Italian-style villa, the museum complex is widely considered one of the best fine arts destinations in Alexandria. A visit to this museum transports guests back to the 1940s and 50s, perfectly encapsulating the golden era of 20th-century Egypt. Museum Components: The complex is structurally divided into three distinct museums and exhibition halls: Mahmoud Saeed Museum (Ground Floor): Displays around 40 of his most significant oil paintings, representing a major pillar of modern plastic art in Egypt. Seif & Adham Wanly Museum (Upper Floor): Dedicated to the artworks of the renowned Alexandrian artist brothers.
